preamble: The shift from the voiced velar stop [ɡ] to the voiced velar fricative [ɣ] (gamma) is a classic form of lenition known as spirantization. It most frequently occurs in intervocalic or post-vocalic positions where the closure required for a stop is incomplete, allowing continuous voiced airflow.

examples.note: Historical spirantization of initial voiced stops in most Dutch dialects.
In Khalkha Mongolian, /g/ is realized as [ɣ] in intervocalic position and between sonorants. The stop allophone [ɡ] appears word-initially and after nasals.
Intervocalic /g/ spirantizes to [ɣ] in medial consonant clusters when preceded by a vowel. Attested across Khalkha and Inner Mongolian varieties.

Kabyle spirantization: lax /g/ in post-vocalic position weakens to the voiced velar fricative [ɣ]. This is part of the same Northern Berber spirantization rule affecting all voiced stops in lax (non-geminate) positions. Geminate /gg/ remains a stop.

Tarifit (Rif Berber, northern Morocco) participates in the general Northern Berber spirantization: lax /g/ between vowels or after a vowel weakens to the velar fricative [ɣ]. The shift parallels /b/→[β], /d/→[ð], /t/→[θ], /k/→[x] in the same dialects.

sources: Ladefoged, P. (2001). A Course in Phonetics.
Penny, R. (2002). A History of the Spanish Language.
Kossmann, M. G. & Stroomer, H. J. (1997). Berber Phonology. In Kaye (ed.), Phonologies of Asia and Africa. Eisenbrauns.
allophones_note: /ɡ/ surfaces as [ɣ] between vowels or after a vowel in word-medial position, while [ɡ] is preserved in word-initial position before a vowel.
